speaker: interviewer
text: OK. It's the 17th of September 1995. The survivor is Ivan Devai. The interviewer is Vanessa Ring. We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And the interview will be in English. And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. My name is Vanessa Ring, V-A-N-E-- double-S-- A R-I-N-G. And I'm interviewing Ivan Devai, whose original name was Deutsch, D-E-U-T-S-C-H and Devai is D-E-V-A-I. It's the 17th of September, 1995. And we're interviewing in Sydney, Australia. The language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: And camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can we start with your name and how to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Ivan Devai, D-E-V-A-I.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a middle name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Ervin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-R-V-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And have you ever been known by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name was originally Deutsch, D-E-U-T-S-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you change it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I changed it in 1949 in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Partly because I had to sort of say get lost. And partly because it was customary by that time to change German sounding names to something more Hungarians. They couldn't spell the name. It gave problem so I decided to change it then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1932, 2nd of April, in Budapest, Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that makes you how old now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That makes me 66 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we'll start now with your life before the war. First of all, could you tell me-- just give me a brief idea of what Budapest was like before the war through your eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, before the war, I was a reasonably young and Budapest for me was that trams and the wide avenues and the snow in winter and-- and the flowers and gardens and that sort of things in-- in summertime. School, family, going out into the mountains over to Buda with my father, with mother, with their friends and their children. We lived a very normal, civilized middle-class life. My father was working hard. Mother was running the household. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] big family get togethers at my grandma's place, at our place. And that was mostly what happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the extended family was a really big part of your life then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Definitely. Definitely. I was the first grandchild, so I was a lot with my grandparents, who adored me. By that time, they were retired so they had [BACKGROUND NOISE] really time to spend with me, to teach me how to ride the bike, and all that sort of things. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: How close were you to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close. Very close. We were very close knit family. And very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather name was Appel, Appel Fulop, [BACKGROUND NOISE] with the P in the middle what was standing for [? Pincus. ?] He came from a little village up [BACKGROUND NOISE] parts of Hungary. And there the Jewish boys had only the Hebrew name. He only took on Fulop [BACKGROUND NOISE] when he came up to Budapest.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your grandmother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Grandmother's name is Roza [? Stenfeld. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And these were your mother's parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother's. [SIGHS] The grandparents on my father's side, I did not know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who else in your extended family were you particularly close to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were mostly more close to my mother's side. My mother had two sisters and a brother. One of the sisters was married. The other sister was not. She was a kindergarten teacher. She had her own kindergarten. I went to that kindergarten. Later my sister went to that kindergarten. And also my mother's brother, Arpad, who we, too, we were very closely attached. My father's sister lived next door to us with two sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Semmerlei, S-E-- double-M-- E-R-L-E-I, [? Bertie ?] and [? Gizzie ?] and [BACKGROUND NOISE] the children's name was [? Lotsi ?] and [? Pichta. ?] Good Hungarian names all around. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister-- your mother's sisters' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's sister name is Klein [BACKGROUND NOISE] Beszi, Erzsebet-- Erzsebet and the younger one is Magda.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kinds of things would you do together when you gathered together as a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we children usually played out in the backyard, we had-- we had back-- big backyard. And older ones we were sitting inside talking politics and family stories and this and that. It's not a-- not a quite clear picture. Grandma came over and did some special cooking. And the family was together. I think that was the-- the main point of it, not what we're doing. It was just to be together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that normal at that time that the extended family was very important?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So you saying that you had a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I have a sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister name is Marica.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any other brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I-- it was only the two of us. And then [? Bergie's ?] son Peter, who was nearly my sister's age. He was part of the-- he was like a brother to us. We grown up together.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old was your sister? Was she older than you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is four years younger than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you get on very well with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my opinion, yes. She said that I was bossing her too much. She still tells me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, you were saying that you had a large garden. Were you in a house or in an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not a garden. It's just a backyard. Our-- we had our own house. And we had the factory in the back. And originally it was a dairy product factory. And the-- the horse driven carts came into the house. And we had a big backyard where they were able to turn around and get loaded and went off to deliver the milk products. And that was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so your father owned that business?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father owned the business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that normal to have a house to yourself at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In our way, yes. Because-- because that was the business was there and we lived there and it was a business originally where you have to start very early morning when the milk start to come in and then get it produced, pasteurized, and made the milk into-- into cheese and butter or whatever. That was very, very early [LAUGHS] time. I only know about it from the stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a-- you remember being a young boy and watching that kind of thing going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No. No. No. No. No. Only later when that factory, the license was taken away, then my father started to repair and make machinery and milk cans for the industry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that that that happened?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it taken away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know exactly, something has to do with Jewish laws and problems with the suppliers who were not very happy to supply Jews. I don't-- I haven't got a clear answer on that. That was too early in my time.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was then able to start up another business?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was able. My father was a very able man and a very hard working man. And he had his connection. And he was very well-liked in the trade. And that's why he was able to start working with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me a bit about your father? He was able. What-- what else can you say about him? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a gentleman, a Hungarian gentleman in appearance, in-- in way of behaving. He had a high school. He had beautiful writing. He was a good businessman. Apparently, even from POW camp, he came back with things what he got together in-- in Siberia, his way home, home, through China.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean got together?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was dealing in gold and-- and brought back gold coins and the rings. And he was spotting an artisan there and he made up gold rings and-- and sold them and that sort of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in the First World War?

speaker: interviewee
text: First World War, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get on with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got on very well with my father. I was a very young boy. I looked up to him very much. But he was working very hard. We did not spend so much time together because he was working. It was more Mom's business to-- to look after the kids and to keep us entertained and take us to school or to-- to other children's birthday parties and that sort of things. Father was the figure who arrived for dinner and had to have his peace and read his newspaper and-- and read-- and drink his tea in the Russian way with the half sugar between his teeth. This I remember. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He's-- And you said that he was a Hungarian gentleman.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a Hungarian gentleman. He came from Heves Megye, what was the most Hungarian and Hungarian area in Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he feel very loyal to Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely. He was a military man. He served his time and with distinction. And he was very Hungarian Hungarian. But he was Israelite and Shabbat was strictly kept with Friday evening dinner and challah and no light switching and no smoking and no writing and all these things. He had his first low seat in the synagogue. And very strictly Israelite. And he was eating with his hat on. And make his [INAUDIBLE] and washed his hand, and we have Pesach and Rosh Hashana and all the main holy days with all the things what goes with it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a traditional Jewish upbringing that you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Definitely. Definitely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that was-- it was fairly strict in terms of being?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, I didn't-- I didn't-- I couldn't say strict or not strict because that was the only thing I-- I know. That was what I seen at my grandparents' at my aunties' at my uncles'. That was the thing. We didn't eat out, only in a fish restaurant or in a number of-- of kosher restaurants, what was available in Budapest. And I think that was one of the reason why the family kept together, and we went to each other place because we knew we that we have the right sort of food as it's supposed to be.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you describe a typical Shabbat evening for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Typical Shabbat evening was that dad came in and everybody was washed and dressed and Mom put up the usual Shabbat dinner. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Soup, chicken soup, naturally, and chicken and-- and Shabbat had a special sauce. What we usually had on the Friday evening. And there was the two challah. And that made the Motzi and the Kaddish for the-- for the wine. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain Motzi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Motzi is-- Motzi, cutting of the challah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were saying--

speaker: interviewee
text: And you giving a piece to everybody.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were saying that you couldn't touch the electric lights.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had the Shabbos [? goyter ?] who did that sort of jobs to switch on and off a light.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what is the Shabbos [? goyter? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: He's a-- he's a non-Jewish person who allowed to do that sort of things. We had a living girl who her mom were running the houseld. She was doing that sort of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any major-- or major recollections of going to synagogue and-- and the High Holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Our-- we-- we lived in-- in Lubowitz [? Motzo, ?] what is on the Eighth District. And four or five corners away was the synagogue in [? Baroshutso. ?] And that's where we always went, actually walking. That was walking distance easily. And the synagogue wasn't a-- a full building. It was in a-- in a flat house. One floor of the flat house was broken slew and separate men's part and the woman strictly, kept away with a wall. And we had an old, for me, an old rabbi, Rabbi Stern. He was also--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you recall of him?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was also my teacher in the elementary school, teaching Jewish things. And he came to our house many times because also my uncle who lived next door, [? Bertie ?] was very religious. And that was our synagogue. That's where we went. There was a big yard outside and all the kids, we were just running up and down there. And when that became very angry, then he grabbed me and made me sit down between him and [? Bertie ?] [? Bachi. ?] and that was the High Holy Days.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Baroshutso ?] Synagogue, nothing special. That was a reasonably large family synagogue, not one of the big built synagogues. And I remember before Yom Kippur to go out next to us was a big garden like-- like a botanical garden. And there where we feeding the fishes before Yom Kippur and the [INAUDIBLE], the chicken, with the chickens and taking it to the butcher to get it cut after.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they sound like happy memories?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they happy times?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you just tell me, can we go back and can you tell me a bit about your mother and what you remember of her at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, my mother was a little lady even then, reasonably well fed. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we lived about three houses from the corner. The corner was the bus stop. In many cases, my mother took a taxi when she went over to Auntie [? Bergie ?] or went into the city because she just couldn't walk because her feet was hurting and all that. It's still hurting thanks goodness.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now you were saying that you had a live-in servant, so-- and it was a fairly middle class family. So you were fairly well off. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. We had what we needed. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I went to-- to the Jewish high school. What was an expensive exercise. We dressed well. We eat well. Some we-- we went on holidays for two, three weeks. Don't ask me where. To a lake. I don't remember. But we went. They came with big horse-drawn carriage and took the things away. And my father once made me a boat about that size to play with it on the lake. They were happy times I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went away on holiday, was that with your whole family? That you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was mother, Marica, me, usually her sister Beszi was coming with Peter too. And then the husbands just came down for the weekend.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a very active lifestyle?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. It was very in the-- in the-- in the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're saying that you went to a Jewish high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you gone to Jewish primary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Primary school I went just next door was the normal Hungarian elementary school. I did four years there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Duganitchuzi ?] Elementary School. My cousins went there. There was a lot of Jewish kids there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever mix with non-Jewish children there?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the elementary school, sure. Sure. With a number of-- a large number of them. But also there was a big difference because next door to us was a building full of it-- very high ranking army officers and their children came to that school. On the other side of the street was the Ludovico Academia, what is a Hungarian officers' school. And the commander of that school and many of the teachers, high ranking officers, lived just next door to us. Many of their kids were our friends. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you got on well with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. At that time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How assimilated was your family into the non-Jewish Hungarian population?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think my father had any close friends. He had many friends from-- from the army and from POW times. Very, rarely he went-- there was a very strong organization [? Frongtard ?] [? Shoshevtiz, ?] what is something like the RSL here, the returned soldiers, and my father very proudly wore its emblem. And he was a member there. I don't think he went there too many times. He was working too hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And in the primary school, did you ever experience anti-semitism at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Because in that school, there was too many Jewish. Quite a number of Jewish teachers still. So it was kept on a-- on a very under level. And we're talking-- we're talking early years, we're talking 1938. It-- it-- not really.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say it was kept on a very underneath level, does that mean that you did sense something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, there was always a couple of big mouths. But generally, it was frowned upon. And-- and we were Hungarians and we stood up and we-- we sing the Himnusz. And the only difference was that we went to a different class when it came to religious education.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in high school, you went to a Jewish high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that time, did you have much contact with non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not. Not.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about outside school, did you ever experience anti-semitism outside school then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, we had a school cap naturally. And it had a big I in it, what meant Israel-- Israelite, RG, Realgymnasium. And we always said only that this reformatus gymnasium. We didn't-- we ignored the I on it because we had to wear the hat, and we didn't want to be too much recognized us as Israelite people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you took the I off, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. No. No. We just ignored it. We said can't you see, it's RG, it's reformat gymnasium.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you ever get picked on for having those hats?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I would recall.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about outside school, did you belong to any groups?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only group I belonged to then was the-- the scout group. That was a Jewish scout group from the-- from the school, 297 [INAUDIBLE] USF scout group. And by that time, Jewish scout group wasn't a lot. So were behind the cover of our Red Cross group. But we did the normal scout things. And instead of the scout symbol, we had a little emblem what just said 297.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when you joined that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 10 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you belong to it for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Till-- till the school was on-- the school, I think, lost somewhere in '44, March, end of March, the school closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So people were aware of its existence, it just wasn't actually known as a scout group. Is that right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kinds of things would you do in the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we did our touring, we did our learning of scout laws and scout things. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that it would have differed at all from any other scout group?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. The only thing that we not allowed to wear uniform, scout uniform. We didn't have scout flag. That was the difference. But we kept up the scout-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] what the scout groups do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And would you say that as time wore on, and you said that you were in it until 1944, that it became something that was a source of strength for you? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It kept us together and kept us occupied. And gave us a chance to-- to fill in our hearts, whatever we wouldn't have anything to do and keep up the spirits. And we thought that if we come, then we can join the real scout movement.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had hope at that time the situation wouldn't get too bad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you form any special friendships in the group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Few. Few. [? Tommy ?] [? Shay, ?] [? Gabby ?] [? Singer ?] names come-- come to mind. But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a loose, quite large group. And it was basically the same group I've seen in school.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So overall, how would you describe those early years when you look back on them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They're very-- I think they were very peaceful, well-balanced, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very normal times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any concerns about the future at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. Not at all. Not at all. It wasn't talked about. It wasn't-- no, no. Don't forget in-- in 1944, I was only 12 years old. And for a 12-year-old as long as I had my good food and my chocolate and my books and my friends and mommy and daddy around and grandpa and we can go out on the weekend to [INAUDIBLE] and up in the mountains and have a good time, I don't think we had any special concerns.

speaker: interviewer
text: So even when your father had to close down his business? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Look, father closed down the business somewhere in '34 or '35. I was eight years old. By the time I started to-- to know what's happening, we had a factory that was working with six, eight people. And even saw that he worked very hard. And my mom, many times, was screaming out to her to the back to [INAUDIBLE], that's enough, come in, finish it. You know? Life was-- looked very, very normal.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. We'll leave it there. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape two of the interview with Ivan Devai. It's the 17th of September 1995. And we're in Sydney, Australia.
